summary: This PR adds a `requests.JSONDecodeError` exception type and wraps `json.JSONDecodeError` in `response.json()`, aiming to provide a consistent exception type regardless of whether `simplejson` is installed. However, the implementation has several critical correctness bugs — most notably a name collision that breaks `json.dumps` calls, incomplete wrapping of JSON errors in the encoding-detection path, Python 2 incompatibility, and a test suite that makes live network requests and unconditionally imports an optional dependency.

findings.title: Renaming `complexjson` import to `json` causes name collision, breaking json.dumps() at runtime
findings.detail: The previous alias `complexjson` was deliberately chosen to avoid a name collision with the `json` parameter in method signatures such as `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)`. Renaming the module import to bare `json` means that inside `prepare_body`, the local parameter `json` shadows the module-level `json` name. At line 476, `json.dumps(json, allow_nan=False)` resolves the first `json` as the local parameter (the user-supplied object), not the module — causing an `AttributeError` at runtime whenever a caller passes a JSON body. This is a critical regression introduced solely by this rename. The original `complexjson` alias (or any private alias such as `_json`) should be retained to preserve the intended scoping.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError does not inherit from RequestException, breaking the stated catch invariant
findings.detail: The PR summary claims that catching `requests.RequestException` will also catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`. This is false: `JSONDecodeError` only inherits from `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` — neither of which is `RequestException`. Any user who writes `except requests.RequestException` will silently miss `requests.JSONDecodeError`, directly contradicting the documented behavior. `JSONDecodeError` must be added to the `RequestException` hierarchy, or the documentation must be corrected and the false promise removed.

findings.title: except json.JSONDecodeError fails on Python 2 where json.JSONDecodeError does not exist
findings.detail: `json.JSONDecodeError` does not exist in Python 2's `json` module; only `ValueError` is raised. On Python 2, the `except json.JSONDecodeError` clause will raise an `AttributeError` at the point of evaluation, completely breaking the `json()` method for Python 2 users. The code should catch `ValueError` (or the `StandardJSONDecodeError` compat alias already established in `exceptions.py`) to be Python 2 safe, mirroring the fallback already present in the exceptions file.

findings.title: Unconditional import of simplejson causes ImportError in environments without simplejson installed
findings.detail: `import simplejson` at line 2 is unconditional. Since the entire PR is motivated by making behavior consistent regardless of whether `simplejson` is installed, the test suite must not require it as a hard dependency. In any environment without `simplejson`, the whole test module fails to import with `ImportError`, making the test suite broken for the majority of users. The import should be guarded with `pytest.importorskip('simplejson')` or a `try/except ImportError` block, and simplejson-specific assertions should be skipped when the library is absent.

findings.title: Tests make live network requests instead of mocked responses, making them unreliable and environment-dependent
findings.detail: All tests in `test_json.py` perform live HTTP GET requests to `httpbin.org` and `google.com`. This makes the test suite non-deterministic: it will fail in offline or restricted-network CI environments, is subject to external service availability and rate limiting, and may carry implicit credentials via proxy/environment config. The test for `test_json_decode_failure_catch` also issues four separate network requests inside one test. These should be rewritten as unit tests using mocked responses (e.g., `unittest.mock`, the `responses` library, or the existing test fixtures/infrastructure in the project). The core new behavior — wrapping `json.JSONDecodeError` as `requests.JSONDecodeError` — has no isolated, deterministic unit test.

findings.title: JSON decode errors from the UTF-detected encoding path are not wrapped and propagate as raw json.JSONDecodeError
findings.detail: In the `json()` method, when content encoding is guessed via `guess_json_utf`, `json.loads` is called (line 905) but only `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught; any `json.JSONDecodeError` raised in this path (e.g., valid UTF-8 but invalid JSON) propagates as the raw `json.JSONDecodeError`, bypassing the normalization this PR intends to guarantee. Only the final `json.loads(self.text)` call (lines 915–920) is wrapped. This makes the fix incomplete and inconsistent: callers who catch `requests.JSONDecodeError` will silently miss errors originating from the encoding-detection branch. Both `json.loads` call sites must be wrapped in the same `try/except` pattern that raises `requests.JSONDecodeError`.

findings.title: SimpleJSONDecodeError falls back to bare Exception, causing unexpected MRO and over-broad inheritance
findings.detail: When `simplejson` is not installed, `SimpleJSONDecodeError` is set to `Exception` (line 19). `JSONDecodeError` then directly inherits from `Exception`, which is an unnecessarily broad base class. While `except JSONDecodeError` still works correctly, the over-broad inheritance is surprising and potentially dangerous. If `StandardJSONDecodeError` also resolves ultimately to `Exception` (Python 2 without simplejson), the MRO is degenerate. The fallback should use a private sentinel base (e.g., `class _NoOpBase: pass`) rather than `Exception` itself to avoid this over-broadening.

findings.title: No tests cover the Python 2 or simplejson-absent fallback paths in the exception hierarchy
findings.detail: The `except ImportError: StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError` branch (lines 13–14) and the `except ImportError: SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception` branch (lines 18–19) represent critical compatibility paths that are entirely untested. There is no test verifying that `requests.JSONDecodeError` is correctly catchable as `ValueError` when `json.JSONDecodeError` is unavailable, nor any test verifying the class hierarchy is sound when simplejson is absent. Because `test_json.py` requires simplejson to be installed, the simplejson-absent path is never exercised by the test suite.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError MRO on Python 2 without simplejson is degenerate and unexpected
findings.detail: On Python 2 without `simplejson`, `StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception`. `class JSONDecodeError(ValueError, Exception)` is technically valid Python but results in a confusing MRO where `JSONDecodeError` is effectively just a `ValueError` subclass with a redundant `Exception` parent. Users expecting to catch `json.JSONDecodeError` (which doesn't exist on Python 2) will be confused, and the class loses its explicit identity. This interacts with the SimpleJSONDecodeError fallback issue and should be addressed together by using a no-op private sentinel base instead of `Exception`.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError raised without exception chaining, discarding the original traceback
findings.detail: `raise JSONDecodeError(e.msg, e.doc, e.pos)` discards the original exception chain. Using `raise JSONDecodeError(e.msg, e.doc, e.pos) from e` would preserve `__cause__` and the original traceback, which is important for debugging and audit logging. Without chaining, tools inspecting `__cause__` or `__context__` will not see the original `json.JSONDecodeError`.

findings.title: No test verifies JSONDecodeError is importable from the top-level requests namespace
findings.detail: `JSONDecodeError` is exported from `requests/__init__.py`, but no test (e.g., in `test_packages.py` or `test_requests.py`) asserts that `requests.JSONDecodeError` exists and is the correct type, independently of the network-dependent `test_json.py`. A simple smoke test should be added.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError docstring style inconsistent with surrounding exceptions
findings.detail: All other exception classes in this file use a full sentence ending with a period and proper capitalisation (e.g., "An HTTP error occurred."). The new `JSONDecodeError` docstring "Couldn't decode the text into json" lacks a terminal period and uses lowercase "json". It should read something like "A JSON decoding error occurred." to match the existing style.

findings.title: Intermediate alias names StandardJSONDecodeError / SimpleJSONDecodeError leak into the public module namespace
findings.detail: `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` are implementation details used only as bases for `JSONDecodeError` and are not part of the public API. They should be prefixed with an underscore (e.g., `_StandardJSONDecodeError`, `_SimpleJSONDecodeError`) to communicate their private nature and avoid polluting the module's public namespace, consistent with the convention of not exposing implementation details at the module level.

findings.title: Import ordering: new import breaks existing alphabetical/grouping convention
findings.detail: The addition of `import json` in `compat.py` is placed before `import chardet`, breaking the existing grouping order (third-party imports like `chardet` are currently listed before stdlib imports like `sys`). The new import should be positioned consistently with the existing grouping pattern.

findings.title: Test URLs are magic strings and inline comments don't align with project conventions
findings.detail: The variables `success_url` and `failure_url` use inline comments as the only explanation. Other test files in the project rely on local mock servers or fixtures rather than live external URLs. Additionally, the comment on the try/except block in `models.py` (lines 918–919) is verbose and restates what the code already shows — a single short comment like `# Re-raise as requests.JSONDecodeError for consistency` would better match the surrounding terse style.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError does not inherit from RequestException, breaking the stated invariant
findings.detail: The PR summary states that `requests.RequestException` should be catchable for `requests.JSONDecodeError`, but `JSONDecodeError` only inherits from `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError`. It does NOT inherit from `RequestException`. The summary explicitly says "all five error types … and its parent class `requests.RequestException`, will be caught from the `requests.JSONDecodeError`" — this is simply false as written. If a user catches `requests.RequestException` they will NOT catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`.

findings.title: SimpleJSONDecodeError falls back to Exception, making JSONDecodeError inherit from bare Exception
findings.detail: When `simplejson` is not installed, `SimpleJSONDecodeError` is set to `Exception`. `JSONDecodeError` then inherits from both `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `Exception`. This is a very broad base class — `except Exception` will always catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`, which is a drastic over-broadening of the catch surface. More importantly, if both `simplejson` is absent AND we're on Python 2 (where `StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError`), the MRO becomes `JSONDecodeError -> ValueError -> Exception`, which is arguably tolerable, but on Python 3 without `simplejson` it's `JSONDecodeError -> json.JSONDecodeError -> ValueError -> Exception`, inheriting from bare `Exception` needlessly. The fallback should use a non-Exception sentinel (e.g., a dummy private base class that doesn't extend `Exception`) to avoid this.

findings.title: JSON decode errors from the UTF-detected path are silently swallowed instead of being re-raised as JSONDecodeError
findings.detail: In the fast path (lines 904-913), when `encoding` is detected via `guess_json_utf`, `json.loads` is called but only `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught. If `json.loads` raises `json.JSONDecodeError` in this path (e.g. the content is valid UTF-8 but is not valid JSON), execution falls through to the `self.text` path and the error is silently ignored. In the old code this was the same behaviour, but the intent of this PR is to always raise `requests.JSONDecodeError`. A `json.JSONDecodeError` from the UTF-detected path should also be caught and re-raised as `requests.JSONDecodeError`.

findings.title: json.JSONDecodeError does not exist on Python 2; catch will fail at runtime
findings.detail: `except json.JSONDecodeError` on line 917 references `json.JSONDecodeError`, which does not exist in Python 2's `json` module (only `ValueError` is raised). On Python 2 this line will raise `AttributeError` at import time or at the point of the `except` clause, completely breaking the `json()` method for Python 2 users. The code should catch `ValueError` (or `StandardJSONDecodeError` from compat) instead, mirroring the fallback already established in `exceptions.py`.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError MRO conflict when both bases are ValueError (Python 2 without simplejson)
findings.detail: On Python 2 without `simplejson`, `StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception`. `JSONDecodeError(ValueError, Exception)` is valid Python, but this is an unexpected MRO and it loses the explicit `JSONDecodeError` identity entirely — users catching `json.JSONDecodeError` on Python 2 would never match because `json.JSONDecodeError` does not exist. The class effectively becomes just a subclass of `ValueError` with an extra `Exception` parent, which may lead to unintended catch behaviour.

findings.title: Test file unconditionally imports simplejson, causing ImportError when simplejson is not installed
findings.detail: Line 2 does `import simplejson` unconditionally. Since simplejson is an optional dependency, this test file will fail with `ImportError` on any environment where simplejson is not installed, making the test suite broken for the majority of users who do not have simplejson. The import should be guarded or use `pytest.importorskip('simplejson')`.

findings.title: Test makes multiple live network requests inside a single test, and duplicate-request structure is incorrect
findings.detail: Each `pytest.raises` block in `test_json_decode_failure_catch` makes a separate live HTTP GET to google.com. More critically, the first `pytest.raises(json.JSONDecodeError)` block will raise and exit after the first exception is caught — meaning only the first `pytest.raises` context actually fires; but since `pytest.raises` is a context manager, each block is independent and each makes its own real network call. This is fragile (network-dependent, rate-limited) and also means the test is making 4 separate network requests. On a CI system without network access these will all fail.

findings.title: Name collision: local parameter `json` shadows the imported `json` module
findings.detail: In `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)`, the parameter `json` shadows the module-level `import json` (now imported directly as `json` rather than `complexjson`). Line 476 calls `json.dumps(json, allow_nan=False)` — the first `json` refers to the parameter (a user-supplied object), not the module, so `json.dumps` would fail with `AttributeError` at runtime when `json` (the parameter) is not None. Previously the module was imported as `complexjson` to avoid exactly this collision. This is a critical breakage introduced by renaming the import.

findings.title: SimpleJSONDecodeError falls back to bare Exception, causing overly broad exception catching
findings.detail: When `simplejson` is not installed, `SimpleJSONDecodeError` is set to `Exception` (line 19). Because `JSONDecodeError` inherits from both `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError`, this means `JSONDecodeError` silently inherits from `Exception` directly. Any `except JSONDecodeError` block will still work, but the inverse is more dangerous: code that does `except Exception` (which is already too broad) gains no narrowing here, and — more critically — if someone checks `issubclass(JSONDecodeError, Exception)` expecting that to only be true when simplejson is available, they will be surprised. More importantly, a `JSONDecodeError` instance will match `isinstance(e, Exception)` unconditionally (which is already true for all exceptions), but the real risk is the MRO: if `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception` and `StandardJSONDecodeError` is also ultimately `Exception`, the class definition `class JSONDecodeError(StandardJSONDecodeError, SimpleJSONDecodeError)` may produce MRO conflicts in certain Python versions when the two bases resolve identically to `Exception`. This should be guarded so that the fallback base is a no-op mixin, not `Exception` itself. Consider using a private sentinel base class instead of `Exception`.

findings.title: JSON decode error in the encoding-guessing branch is silently swallowed, exposing the raw library exception
findings.detail: In the `json()` method, when a content-encoding is guessed (lines 902-913), a `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught and execution falls through (pass), but any `json.JSONDecodeError` raised by `json.loads` on line 905 is NOT caught and will propagate as the raw `json.JSONDecodeError`, not as the new `requests.JSONDecodeError`. Only the final `json.loads` call (line 916) is wrapped in the new try/except. This means the fix is incomplete: the early-return path still raises the inconsistent exception types the PR claims to fix. An attacker or a server returning crafted content could cause the guessed-encoding path to trigger, bypassing the normalisation. This is a correctness/security-surface inconsistency: callers relying on catching `requests.JSONDecodeError` will miss errors from this path.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError raised without chaining the original exception (loses cause)
findings.detail: The `raise JSONDecodeError(e.msg, e.doc, e.pos)` on line 920 discards the original exception chain. Using `raise JSONDecodeError(...) from e` would preserve the traceback and exception cause, which is important for security/audit logging and debugging. Without chaining, tools that inspect `__cause__` or `__context__` will not see the original error.

findings.title: Test file makes live network requests to external hosts — CI secret/environment leakage risk
findings.detail: The test file issues real HTTP GET requests to `https://httpbin.org/get` and `https://google.com` (lines 7-8, 12, 17, 21, 23, 26). This creates several security concerns: (1) In CI environments, outbound HTTP requests can carry implicit credentials (proxy auth, corporate SSO cookies, environment tokens captured in User-Agent or headers set by the requests library). (2) The test outcome depends on external services outside the project's control; a malicious or compromised `httpbin.org` response could influence test behavior. (3) The hard dependency on `simplejson` (line 2, `import simplejson`) will fail if simplejson is not installed, but more importantly the test asserts `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` is catchable (line 20-21) — if simplejson is absent this test crashes rather than being skipped, potentially hiding the fact that the fallback `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception` path is untested. These tests should use mocked/local responses instead of live network calls.

findings.title: Hard unconditional import of simplejson in test file
findings.detail: `import simplejson` on line 2 is unconditional. If `simplejson` is not installed (which is a supported configuration per the PR's own logic), the entire test module will fail to import with an `ImportError`, rather than gracefully skipping simplejson-specific tests. This should be wrapped in a try/except with a pytest skip or `pytest.importorskip`.

findings.title: Docstring style inconsistent with existing exceptions
findings.detail: All other exception classes in this file use a full sentence ending with a period (e.g. "An HTTP error occurred.", "A Connection error occurred."). The new `JSONDecodeError` docstring "Couldn't decode the text into json" lacks a period and uses lowercase "json" — it should read something like "A JSON decoding error occurred." to match the surrounding style.

findings.title: Intermediate alias names `StandardJSONDecodeError` / `SimpleJSONDecodeError` are file-level names that leak into the module namespace
findings.detail: The names `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` are only needed as bases for `JSONDecodeError` and are not part of the public API. Existing convention in this file is to import names directly without intermediate aliases. Prefixing them with an underscore (e.g. `_StandardJSONDecodeError`, `_SimpleJSONDecodeError`) would communicate their private nature and match the module's implicit convention of not exposing implementation details at the module level.

findings.title: Import ordering: `import json` placed before `import chardet` breaks the existing alphabetical/grouping order
findings.detail: In the existing file, stdlib imports (`import sys`) come after third-party imports (`import chardet`). Moving `import json` to after `import chardet` (or grouping all stdlib imports together below the third-party ones) would be consistent with the existing ordering pattern in the file.

findings.title: Renaming `complexjson` import to `json` shadows the stdlib `json` name unexpectedly
findings.detail: The previous alias `complexjson` was deliberately chosen to avoid a name collision with the `json` parameter used in several method signatures in this file (e.g. `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)`). Renaming it to bare `json` means the local parameter `json` in those methods shadows the module-level `json` name. At line 476, `json.dumps(json, allow_nan=False)` works only because `json` (the module) is referenced before the local `json` parameter takes effect in that scope, but the naming is confusing and inconsistent with the deliberate choice made in the original code. A clearer alias like `complexjson` or `_json` should be retained.

findings.title: `import simplejson` at the top level makes simplejson a hard test dependency
findings.detail: The PR's whole point is that `simplejson` is optional. Importing it unconditionally at the top of the test file will cause an `ImportError` in environments where `simplejson` is not installed, making the test suite fail. This is also inconsistent with how optional dependencies are handled elsewhere in the codebase (try/except import). Even from a pure style standpoint, an unconditional import of an optional library is inconsistent with the surrounding convention.

findings.title: Test URLs are undocumented magic strings; no comments explaining their role
findings.detail: The variable names `success_url` and `failure_url` are reasonable, but the inline comments ("# returns JSON" and "# doesn't return JSON") are the only explanation. Looking at other test files in the project, tests typically use local mock servers or fixtures rather than live external URLs. While that is a correctness/reliability concern, from a style perspective the names and comments don't align with the convention visible in the existing test files.

findings.title: Comment on line 918-919 is verbose and restates what the code already shows
findings.detail: The comment "# Catch JSON-related errors and raise as requests.JSONDecodeError / # This aliases json.JSONDecodeError and simplejson.JSONDecodeError" explains implementation details that are already documented in the exception class itself and in the docstring above. Existing comments in this file are terse and describe *why*, not *what*. A single short comment like `# Re-raise as requests.JSONDecodeError for consistency` would match the surrounding style better.

findings.title: Tests make real network requests instead of using mocks/fixtures
findings.detail: All tests in test_json.py perform live HTTP requests to httpbin.org and google.com. This makes them integration tests that require network access and external service availability. Unit tests for `response.json()` should use mocked responses (e.g., via `unittest.mock`, `responses` library, or the existing test infrastructure in conftest.py/testserver). These tests will fail in offline/CI environments and are non-deterministic.

findings.title: Test file unconditionally imports simplejson, making tests fail when simplejson is not installed
findings.detail: `import simplejson` at line 2 will raise ImportError if simplejson is not installed. Since the entire PR is about making behavior consistent regardless of whether simplejson is installed, the test suite itself should not require simplejson as a hard dependency. The test for `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` catchability should be skipped or conditionalized when simplejson is absent.

findings.title: No test covers the Python 2 fallback path (StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError)
findings.detail: The `except ImportError: StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError` branch (line 13-14) represents a key compatibility path for Python 2. There is no test that verifies `requests.JSONDecodeError` is catchable as `ValueError` in an environment where `json.JSONDecodeError` does not exist. The existing test at line 26 of test_json.py does assert `ValueError` is raised, but relies on a live network call and does not isolate this branch.

findings.title: No test covers the simplejson-absent fallback path (SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception)
findings.detail: The `except ImportError: SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception` branch (lines 18-19) is only exercised if simplejson is not installed. There is no test that verifies the class hierarchy is still correct (and doesn't accidentally make `requests.JSONDecodeError` a subclass of the base `Exception` in an unexpected way) when simplejson is absent. test_json.py requires simplejson to be installed, so this path is never tested.

findings.title: UnicodeDecodeError path in json() does not raise requests.JSONDecodeError — no test for the new control-flow difference
findings.detail: In the updated `json()` method, when `encoding` is detected and `json.loads` raises a `UnicodeDecodeError`, execution falls through (`pass`) to the outer `try/except json.JSONDecodeError` block (lines 915-920). However, when the outer `json.loads` call itself raises a `UnicodeDecodeError`, that exception is NOT caught by the new `except json.JSONDecodeError` handler and will propagate as a raw `UnicodeDecodeError`, not as `requests.JSONDecodeError`. There is no test covering the case where the fallback `json.loads(self.text)` raises a `UnicodeDecodeError`. This is a coverage gap for the new try/except block's exception handling boundary.

findings.title: No unit test for the new try/except JSONDecodeError block in response.json()
findings.detail: The core new behavior — wrapping `json.JSONDecodeError` into `requests.JSONDecodeError` — has no isolated unit test using a mocked response with invalid JSON content. The only tests in test_json.py use live network calls to google.com, which may or may not return invalid JSON depending on the response encoding path taken. There is no test that exercises the specific code path at lines 915-920 with a controlled invalid-JSON response body.

findings.title: No test for the encoding-detected json.loads path raising JSONDecodeError
findings.detail: The inner `json.loads` call at line 905 (within the encoding-detection branch) can also raise a `json.JSONDecodeError`, but this exception is NOT caught and re-raised as `requests.JSONDecodeError` — it propagates as-is. This inconsistency compared to the outer path is not covered by any test.

findings.title: No test for successful json() decoding using a mocked/local response
findings.detail: `test_json_decode_success()` only verifies a live network response from httpbin.org returns a dict. There is no offline unit test verifying that `response.json()` correctly returns decoded JSON content from a controlled response body, making it impossible to verify the method's logic without network access.

findings.title: No test verifies that JSONDecodeError is importable from the top-level requests namespace
findings.detail: `JSONDecodeError` is now exported from `requests/__init__.py`. There is no test (e.g., in test_packages.py or test_requests.py) that simply asserts `requests.JSONDecodeError` exists and is the correct type, separate from the network-dependent test_json.py.

findings.title: Renaming `complexjson` import to `json` causes name collision, breaking json.dumps() at runtime
findings.detail: The previous alias `complexjson` was deliberately chosen to avoid a name collision with the `json` parameter in method signatures such as `prepare_body(self, data, files, json=None)`. Renaming the module import to bare `json` means that inside `prepare_body`, the local parameter `json` shadows the module-level `json` name. At line 476, `json.dumps(json, allow_nan=False)` resolves the first `json` as the local parameter (the user-supplied object), not the module — causing an `AttributeError` at runtime whenever a caller passes a JSON body. This is a critical regression introduced solely by this rename. The original `complexjson` alias (or any private alias such as `_json`) should be retained to preserve the intended scoping.

findings.detail: The PR summary claims that catching `requests.RequestException` will also catch `requests.JSONDecodeError`. findings.detail: `import simplejson` at line 2 is unconditional. In any environment without `simplejson`, the whole test module fails to import with `ImportError`, making the test suite broken for the majority of users. The test for `test_json_decode_failure_catch` also issues four separate network requests inside one test. The core new behavior — wrapping `json.JSONDecodeError` as `requests.JSONDecodeError` — has no isolated, deterministic unit test.

The fallback should use a private sentinel base (e.g., `class _NoOpBase: pass`) rather than `Exception` itself to avoid this over-broadening.

findings.title: No tests cover the Python 2 or simplejson-absent fallback paths in the exception hierarchy
findings.detail: The `except ImportError: StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError` branch (lines 13–14) and the `except ImportError: SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception` branch (lines 18–19) represent critical compatibility paths that are entirely untested. There is no test verifying that `requests.JSONDecodeError` is correctly catchable as `ValueError` when `json.JSONDecodeError` is unavailable, nor any test verifying the class hierarchy is sound when simplejson is absent. Because `test_json.py` requires simplejson to be installed, the simplejson-absent path is never exercised by the test suite.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError MRO on Python 2 without simplejson is degenerate and unexpected
findings.detail: On Python 2 without `simplejson`, `StandardJSONDecodeError = ValueError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError = Exception`. `class JSONDecodeError(ValueError, Exception)` is technically valid Python but results in a confusing MRO where `JSONDecodeError` is effectively just a `ValueError` subclass with a redundant `Exception` parent. Users expecting to catch `json.JSONDecodeError` (which doesn't exist on Python 2) will be confused, and the class loses its explicit identity. This interacts with the SimpleJSONDecodeError fallback issue and should be addressed together by using a no-op private sentinel base instead of `Exception`.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError raised without exception chaining, discarding the original traceback
findings.detail: `raise JSONDecodeError(e.msg, e.doc, e.pos)` discards the original exception chain. Using `raise JSONDecodeError(e.msg, e.doc, e.pos) from e` would preserve `__cause__` and the original traceback, which is important for debugging and audit logging. Without chaining, tools inspecting `__cause__` or `__context__` will not see the original `json.JSONDecodeError`.

findings.title: No test verifies JSONDecodeError is importable from the top-level requests namespace
findings.detail: `JSONDecodeError` is exported from `requests/__init__.py`, but no test (e.g., in `test_packages.py` or `test_requests.py`) asserts that `requests.JSONDecodeError` exists and is the correct type, independently of the network-dependent `test_json.py`. A simple smoke test should be added.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError docstring style inconsistent with surrounding exceptions
findings.detail: All other exception classes in this file use a full sentence ending with a period and proper capitalisation (e.g., "An HTTP error occurred."). The new `JSONDecodeError` docstring "Couldn't decode the text into json" lacks a terminal period and uses lowercase "json". It should read something like "A JSON decoding error occurred." to match the existing style.

findings.title: Intermediate alias names StandardJSONDecodeError / SimpleJSONDecodeError leak into the public module namespace
findings.detail: `StandardJSONDecodeError` and `SimpleJSONDecodeError` are implementation details used only as bases for `JSONDecodeError` and are not part of the public API. They should be prefixed with an underscore (e.g., `_StandardJSONDecodeError`, `_SimpleJSONDecodeError`) to communicate their private nature and avoid polluting the module's public namespace, consistent with the convention of not exposing implementation details at the module level.

findings.title: Import ordering: new import breaks existing alphabetical/grouping convention
findings.detail: The addition of `import json` in `compat.py` is placed before `import chardet`, breaking the existing grouping order (third-party imports like `chardet` are currently listed before stdlib imports like `sys`). The new import should be positioned consistently with the existing grouping pattern.

findings.title: Test URLs are magic strings and inline comments don't align with project conventions
findings.detail: The variables `success_url` and `failure_url` use inline comments as the only explanation. Other test files in the project rely on local mock servers or fixtures rather than live external URLs. Additionally, the comment on the try/except block in `models.py` (lines 918–919) is verbose and restates what the code already shows — a single short comment like `# Re-raise as requests.JSONDecodeError for consistency` would better match the surrounding terse style.
